description: The General Data Protection Regulation was adopted in 2016 and enforced from May 25, 2018. Public awareness grew slowly, then spiked at enforcement. Support was consistently high among those aware (~70-80%), though 'cookie consent fatigue' created a nuanced picture of frustration with implementation despite supporting the principle.

key_events.description: GDPR adopted April 2016. Two-year transition period begins. Low public awareness (~30% of EU citizens). Tech industry begins compliance preparation.
Cambridge Analytica scandal breaks (March 2018). Public awareness of data privacy surges. GDPR framed as timely and necessary. Support reinforced.
GDPR enforcement begins May 25, 2018. Flood of consent emails and cookie pop-ups. Initial novelty — users exercise new rights (data access requests surge). Eurobarometer shows 67% EU citizens aware.
Cookie consent fatigue sets in. Users report clicking 'accept all' without reading. Critics argue GDPR created compliance theater while real data practices unchanged. Some US sites block EU access.
First major fines: Google fined €50M by French CNIL. Principle remains supported but implementation criticized. Eurobarometer 2019: 69% consider data protection rules important.

calibration_notes: High baseline support for privacy principle with slight erosion due to implementation friction. Eurobarometer 2019 showed 69% of EU citizens felt national data protection rules were important. Unique dynamic: people supported GDPR's goals but were annoyed by its daily manifestation (cookie pop-ups). Tests: principle vs. implementation gap, awareness-driven support curves, regulatory fatigue, Cambridge Analytica as exogenous shock reinforcing support.
